ev_152.tag: Moral change through collective action represents learning and improvement toward objective moral standards
ev_152.full_text:
‘slavery necessary’ claims countered by actual abolition that proved it wasn’t
Practical change necessary to bring collective learning
Pure moral beliefs inherently skewed by societal norms
White abolitionists skewed by racism, slaves portrayed as victims of suffering
Black abolitionists portrayed slaves as ppl denied basic respect, freedom, etc, better addressed claims that Black ppl less intelligent
Slaves demanding respect changed white views of them as not needing respect, Black ppl being made soldiers demonstrated they could exercise courage, strength, etc
Abstract moral theorization inherently skewed by biases, must be grounded in empirical testing
Yet, moral beliefs about slavery did change. After the Civil War, while Southern whites insisted on white supremacy, most came to accept sharecropping as superior to slavery.44 The practical success of emancipation led them to drop all of the arguments they had previously made in support of the supposed necessity of slavery. The full story of how this change in moral beliefs came about is too complex for this lecture. Here I stress two major factors. First, to change moral beliefs, slavery had to be challenged not only in pure moral arguments but in practical, collective action. Second, slaves and free blacks had to actively participate in those challenges.¶ In social theory, “contention” refers to practices in which people make claims against others, on behalf of someone’s interests. “Contentious politics” consists of coordinated contention by groups around a shared agenda, involving governments as “targets, initiators of claims, or third parties.”45 Contentious practices span a spectrum from pure moral argument at one end, to riots, war, and other violent acts on the other. Between pure argument and violence is a wide range of contentious activities that are more or less disruptive of habitual ways of life, from petitioning, publicity campaigns, theatrical performances, candlelight vigils, litigation, and political campaigns, to street demonstrations, boycotts, teach-ins, sit-ins, picketing, strikes, building occupations, and other forms of civil disobedience. As people move beyond the pure moral argument pole, they manifest in action and not only words their refusal to go along with the moral norms they are rejecting. Once it gets beyond pure moral argument, contention consists in the collective, concerted repudiation of morally objectionable practices by means of actions that disrupt the routine functioning of those practices, and that express rejection of the moral authority of people to practice them.¶ Contention aims to secure the satisfaction of claims by eliciting the recognition of those in power of the legitimacy of those claims, and thereby the incorporation into social institutions of an established recognition of those claims.46 It might seem that violent acts, on this definition, could not count as contention, even if they have political aims. To be sure, political violence used simply to get one’s way by force, as in cases of genocide and ethnic cleansing, does not address the victims as agents of whom it is demanded that they respond to claims. But other kinds of violence do aim at eliciting the practical recognition from authorities of legitimate claims. For example, the American War of Independence aimed not simply at obtaining de facto independence from Britain but at securing recognition from Britain of the United States as a sovereign nation. The war was a form of violent contention.¶ I claim that, in some circumstances, practical contention brings about collective moral learning—learning on the part of societies—that pure moral argument cannot. We have evidence that moral change induced by contention counts as learning—as an improvement of moral beliefs— if the contention blocks, counteracts, bypasses, or corrects cognitive or moral biases that supported the status quo ante, such that the new moral beliefs embodied in altered practice are not, or at least less, distorted by those biases. In such cases, we have similar grounds for claiming that the new moral beliefs are more reliable as in cases of belief change on the basis of blinded placebo-controlled clinical trials.¶ Practical contention, not just individual moral persuasion, is needed to effect collective moral belief change because collective moral beliefs are embodied in social norms. Social norms are sustained by reciprocal expectations of conditional conformity. They involve tacit or explicit agreements within a society to conform to the norm, on condition that enough others conform. Collective moral beliefs are embodied in social norms of discussion, joint deliberation, and claim-making. A group shares a belief if that belief shapes discourse within the group: the group takes it for granted as a premise for further argument, not needing independent justification; its truth is treated as a settled matter; disputing it is regarded as, if not beyond the pale, requiring a heavy burden of proof; disputants are liable to censure or even social exclusion for calling such convictions into question.47 For belief in a moral principle to be collectively accepted also requires that the principle regulates interpersonal claim-making: members are free to make claims in accordance with the principle and generally do so when they are victimized by violations of it; other members acknowledge the legitimacy of such claims; the principle is widely if not completely obeyed by group members; the group punishes disobedience; members take steps to transmit the principle to future generations.48¶ Because collective moral beliefs are sustained by reciprocal expectations, an individual can privately dissent while still participating in the practices that sustain the belief for the group. Hence, merely changing an individual’s mind through moral argument need not change the collective belief. Furthermore, individuals may resist acting on their personal conclusions because a belief is held collectively. This is not simply because they lack the courage of their convictions. They may wonder whether they have reasoned correctly if they reach conclusions contrary to the group consensus, and think that the group’s belief is more reliable than their own reasoning. Pure moral argument may also lack a certain degree of seriousness, insofar as it is advanced in contexts outside of interpersonal claim-making, by people who lack direct stakes in what they are saying.¶ Contentious politics avoids these weaknesses of pure moral argument. In contentious political practices, people advance moral beliefs in the context of actual claim-making: the stakes are real and serious. Because these practices involve mass action in public repudiation of existing norms, they destabilize[s] the shared expectations that hold those norms in place, casting doubt on the robustness or authenticity of the purported consensus around them. Their mass public nature may give courage to those who privately dissented, proving that their doubts about existing norms were not merely the product of idiosyncratic reasoning. To the extent that contentious politics disrupts the routine operation of challenged norms, it [and] forces genuine practical deliberation about what to do, not mere idle speculation. In refusing to concede legitimacy to the enforcement of challenged norms, contentious politics threatens a loss of honor on the part of those who do enforce them—something that may inspire the enforcers to reconsider them.49¶ Contentious politics thus serves to awaken societies to serious practical reflection on entrenched moral beliefs. More is needed, however, to ensure that the direction their reflection takes is less biased. Many features of contention can play this role. Here I stress one: the participation of the victims of injustice in challenging the norms that oppress them.¶ So far I have discussed the moral arguments made by white abolitionists such as Hepburn, Clarkson, Weld, and Garrison. As we have seen, their strategies were ineffective against the slaveholding culture of the South. Racism posed powerful obstacles to their efforts. Despite the abstract commitment of white abolitionists to the equality of blacks before God, and hence their equal moral considerability, racism biased their representation of the evils of slavery. They overwhelmingly represented slaves as victims of cruelty and material deprivation. Weld’s American Slavery as It Is (1839) (the inspiration for Harriet Beecher Stowe’s Uncle Tom’s Cabin, which, with Weld’s work, constituted the two most influential white abolitionist publications in the U.S.) documents in exhaustive detail the material deprivations inflicted on slaves and their subjection to cruel tortures. Notably, these wrongs can be suffered equally much by animals. By contrast, Weld’s work passes relatively lightly over slavery’s manifold assaults on slaves’ specifically human, dignitary interests in their agency and in recognition from others: the deprivation of autonomy, legal rights, education, and opportunities for self-advancement; the theft of the fruits of their labor; the dishonor inflicted on female slaves through slaveholder rape; the dishonor imposed on male slaves by denying them authority over family life, powers to protect their wives and children, and access to avenues for developing and exercising military virtues. White abolitionists thus tended to cast slaves more as objects of pity than as subjects of dignity entitled to command respect. They were notably weak in addressing slaveholders’ claims that b[B]lacks lacked intelligence, talent, foresight, and capacities for self-governance, and so would be unable to compete with whites in a free labor market, but sink into destitution, vagrancy, and crime if they were freed—key elements in slaveholders’ patriarchal defense of slavery as necessary for blacks’ welfare and social order.¶ Black abolitionists placed greater emphasis on the ways in which slavery deprived slaves of dignity, honor, and access to distinctively human rights and achievements. The central theme of Harriet Jacobs’s Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl Written by Herself was the vulnerability of slave women to sexual harassment and rape at the hands of their masters. The female slave “is not allowed to have any pride of character. It is deemed a crime in her to wish to be virtuous.” Jacobs rated this injury as far worse than slavery’s material deprivations or consignment to a life of drudgery. She hid in a tiny, dark attic for almost seven years to avoid sexual assault, judging this fate better than slavery, even though she had never been whipped, beaten, or overworked as a slave.50 Frederick Douglass agreed with Jacobs’s priorities. Worse than the whip was slavery’s consignment of slaves to ignorance and incapacity to think for themselves. Indeed, the fundamental point, and greatest injury, of material deprivation and brutal physical punishment was to disable slaves from aspiring to freedom, to the exercise of rational capacities, to any kind of estimable activity.51 From this dignitary perspective, Douglass exposed slaveholders’ boasts of the material indulgence they granted their slaves on holidays, when they were encouraged to get drunk and discouraged from any work, as a great fraud, designed only “to disgust their slaves with freedom, by plunging them into the lowest depths of dissipation.”52¶ Black abolitionists’ alternative critique of the evils of slavery led them to advocate a different strategy for bringing about moral change— one addressed as much to antislavery Northerners as to advocates of slavery. Their critique identified racism—the widespread, deeply entrenched contempt for b[B]lacks, based on prejudicial feelings of their being unfit for freedom and equal dignity with whites—as the core moral bias upholding slavery. To counteract this prejudice, much more than pure moral argument was required. Blacks needed to demonstrate in action their interest, capacity, and worthiness for freedom and dignity. “We . . . wish to see the charges of Mr. Jefferson refuted by the blacks themselves” for, if blacks fail to try, “we will only establish them.”53 As James McCune Smith, the first African-American to earn a medical degree, and editor of Douglass’s My Bondage and My Freedom, put the point:¶ The real object of that [antislavery] movement is not only to disenthrall, it is, also, to bestow upon the Negro the exercise of all those rights, from the possession of which he has been so long debarred. But this full recognition of the colored man to the right, and the entire admission of the same to the full privileges, political, religious and social, of manhood, requires powerful effort on the part of the enthralled, as well as on the part of those who would disenthrall them. The people at large must feel the conviction, as well as admit the abstract logic, of human equality; the Negro . . . must prove his title first to all that is demanded for him; in the teeth of unequal chances, he must prove himself equal to the mass of those who oppress him . . ..54¶ Without such effort by blacks themselves “to disprove their alleged inferiority, and demonstrate their capacity for a more exalted civilization than slavery and prejudice had assigned to them,” whites would “reconcile themselves” to blacks’ “enslavement and oppression, as things inevitable, if not desirable.”55¶ This task stood in tension with white abolitionists’ strategy to present slaves as objects of pity. Douglass grated under their requests that he merely “give us the facts,” and “we will take care of the philosophy.” They implored him to speak to audiences with an uneducated plantation accent, lest Northern whites think he wasn’t really a fugitive slave. They objected to his establishing a paper of his own, preferring that he continue to lecture under their sponsorship, oblivious to the importance Douglass saw in demonstrating blacks’ capacities and inspiring, through his achievements, other blacks to that call.56¶ In this dispute, black abolitionists proved to be far keener moral psychologists than their white counterparts. White abolitionists, in stressing the pathos of slavery, operated on the assumption that the core moral bias of slavery advocates was heard-heartedness. On that assumption, the key strategy for counteracting that bias should be to highlight those facts about slavery that arouse people’s sympathies and to cultivate social practices that encourage sentimentality and open­ heartedness, so that people feel free to respond appropriately to those facts. Black abolitionists identified the core weakness of this strategy: “Human nature is so constituted, that it cannot honor a helpless man, although it can pity him; and even this it cannot do long, if the signs of power do not arise.”57 If the core moral bias of slavery advocates was racist contempt, then this can only be counteracted by resisting subordination and oppression, demanding respect, and seizing it, by force if necessary, from those who withhold it. To demonstrate worthiness of respect, one must conduct oneself as entitled to it. Failing that, the contemptuous will think their targets uninterested in, incapable of, and hence undeserving of respect.¶ On this point, black abolitionists were united. Their writings repeatedly testify to the power of blacks’ standing up for their rights, and the supreme importance of their doing so. Jacobs “resolved never to be conquered” and resisted her master’s sexual advances. Escaping North, she successfully opposed racial discrimination in hotel service by telling the black servants that they should stand up to oppose it.58 Douglass admired the unbowed resistance of Nelly to overseer Mr. Servier’s blows, noting that he never whipped her again.59 This incident prefigured his own triumphant struggle against the slavebreaker Covey, from which he drew his central insight into the moral psychology of overcoming oppression: to obtain recognition of one’s respectability from others, one must manifest self-respect in action by exacting respect from others.¶ This call to resistance was the core of David Walker’s Appeal.60 And resist the slaves did, taking deeds, more than words, as the key to progressive moral change. Slaves exploited the legal codes of the South to extract recognition of rights through innumerable acts of resistance on the plantations, including, in some cases (astonishingly!), the right to kill their masters in self-defense.61 There was no better proof that slaves desired freedom and repudiated enslavement than the steady flow of fugitives North, without regret or reversal. Toward the end of the Civil War, the Confederacy, running out of soldiers, debated whether to draft slaves into the army. Howell Cobb, one of the founders of the Confederacy, answered, “If slaves will make good soldiers our whole theory of slavery is wrong.”62 But fugitive slaves demonstrated, in their courageous service in the Union Army, that slaves did make good soldiers. They thereby heeded Walker’s call for blacks themselves to refute Jefferson’s aspersions on their race and shattered the South’s “whole theory of slavery.” While their actions did not end racism, they did force a momentous retreat of this profound moral bias. Slavery advocates were forced to concede that the case for slavery was spurious, and that blacks were fit at least for the autonomy that the emergent sharecropping economy conceded to them. This was not full freedom by any means, but it was a giant step up from slavery.¶ 4. SOME PRAGMATIST PATHS FORWARD FOR MORAL PHILOSOPHY¶ Let us step back and draw some lessons from this monumental episode of collective moral learning. Recall that pragmatism replaces the quest for ultimate criteria of moral rightness, true in all possible worlds or at least at high levels of abstraction, with methods of intelligent updating. I argued that one important type of intelligent updating involves blocking, counteracting, or reducing the influence of moral biases. We have reasons to believe that social power biases moral reasoning in systematic ways. First, as Smith argued, people tend to feel more sympathy, and more esteem, for the rich and powerful relative to the poor and powerless, controlling for equal suffering and equal merit. The latter unjustly suffer contempt. He could have added that such contempt tends to be rationalized by biased notions of group inferiority. Second, as Dewey and Tufts argued, the powerful—who shape social institutions to benefit their social groups at others’ expense—tend to confuse what they want with what is right so long as they have the power to enforce their demands.¶ Faced merely with pure moral argument, we have seen that the powerful, and their advocates, typically have substantial resources at their disposal, from the intuitive moral ideas and principles available in their society, to rationalize their side of the debate. Nor does purely speculative, a priori moral argument typically activate real practical reasoning. Hence, the powers of pure moral argument to dislodge prejudice and bias tend to be weak.¶ Stronger methods are needed to counteract the biases induced by social power. My case study of a society-wide change in moral belief, from proslavery to abolitionist, focused on two such methods. First, contentious politics—active, practical, mass resistance to the moral claims embodied in social institutions enforced by and catering to the powerful—is needed to activate genuine practical reasoning across all levels of society. The powerful won’t really listen to reason—that is, to claims from below—until they no longer have the power to routinely enforce their desires. Second, the subordinated and oppressed must actively participate in that contention. They must manifest in deed and not only words their own interest, capacity, and worthiness for the rights and privileges they are demanding. For if they meekly submit to oppression, this tends to make observers—not only the powerful, but anyone, as Smith held—think that the downtrodden have no interest in or capacity for uplift and do not deserve it. The oppressed must show their determination to cast off oppression in order to arouse the esteem and thereby enlist the support or at least the acquiescence of others.¶ Walker, Jacobs, McCune, and Douglass understood this. Respect is obtained from others not by abstract argument but by dignified exaction. No wonder Douglass lost all patience for abstract moral argument:¶ [W]here all is plain there is nothing to be argued. . . . Must I undertake to prove that the slave is a man? . . . The slaveholders themselves acknowledge it . . . when they punish disobedience on the part of the slave. What is this but the acknowledgement that the slave is a moral, intellectual, and responsible being . . . [I]t is not light that is needed, but fire. . . . The feeling of the nation must be quickened; the conscience of the nation must be roused; . . . the hypocrisy of the nation must be exposed; and its crimes against God and man must be proclaimed and denounced.63¶ In the language of contemporary moral philosophy, Douglass was calling for a shift from third-person to second-person address, from abstract impersonal argument to interpersonal claim-making, founded on an assertion of authority to demand respect from others.64 To be called to account, to be addressed as a bearer of duties to the addresser, to be upbraided for failure to do what is authoritatively demanded—these are essential experiences needed to become a morally responsible being, fit for living with others. And these are the experiences to which slaveholders, holding irresponsible totalitarian power over slaves, were least exposed before the Civil War. Yet, in the perverse corruption of moral sentiments Smith identified, until the enslaved actively repudiated their subjection, it was the slaves, rather than the slaveholders, who were thought unfit for living freely with others.¶ From our current moral perspective, it is easy for us to see the errors of the past, with respect to slavery. A skeptic might wonder whether we are merely begging the question in favor of our current moral beliefs. The pragmatist answers that this change can be seen to be progressive, a case of moral learning, because it was brought about through practices that tend to counteract or reduce known moral biases rooted in human psychology. As clinical conclusions reached on the basis of blinded, placebo-controlled clinical trials are more reliable, due to the ways they check the biases of wishful thinking, moral conclusions reached on the basis of practical methods that counteract the biases of power are similarly more reliable.¶ This pragmatist perspective suggests an alternative research program for moral philosophy, reaching beyond the a priori methods to which we philosophers are so wedded. My point is to expand the tools we use, and to reduce our excessive reliance on the old tools. Just as a bolt will turn uselessly without a nut to fasten it, or glued joints will be weak if they haven’t been clamped, our abstract moral arguments will spin without conclusion or fall apart uselessly unless they are used in conjunction with empirically grounded tools. We can make better progress by working in close conjunction with the social sciences and history to consider empirically how different circumstances, including social relations, shape our moral thinking. If we discover an influence on our moral thinking that we can’t justify, or that experience shows us to lead to untoward consequences, we have discovered a moral bias. Then we can seek empirically reliable methods to correct, block, counteract, or bypass those biases, keeping in mind that pure reasoning may not be enough. Some methods may be practical, not just speculative or theoretical, and involve concerted action in the world, sometimes collective political action.¶ This alternative research program does not reject intuitions. They are a basic material of moral thinking; we have no way around them. But we must be alert to the possibility that our intuitions might suffer from bias and would be improved under alternative conditions.¶ My case study raises an alarm for philosophy as we currently practice it. Without active participation of the oppressed and disadvantaged, the moral views reached by philosophers are liable to be biased—ignorant of and unresponsive to the concerns and claims of those not present.65 Dewey and Tufts identified that problem, too. Morality, understood as what we owe to each other, arises from the need to adjudicate the claims that everyone makes on everyone else. If the claims of the subordinated are suppressed, silenced, ignored, or misunderstood, the conclusions reached on the basis of the subset of claims that are considered are liable to be systematically biased. My case study indicates that purely a priori methods of bias correction are unlikely to reliably counteract such biases.66 There is no reason to think that ever-more-elaborate exploration of the contours of one’s own moral thoughts, or of the thoughts of similarly situated persons, will capture everyone’s moral concerns. Knowledge of what we owe to each other can only be generated through processes of interpersonal claim-making that include those occupying the full range of diverse situations in society. For moral philosophy to make progress, it must practice inclusion of diverse philosophers.¶ In this lecture, I have focused on bias correction as one basic pragmatist method. Another is experiments in living. The conclusions we reach from real experiments in living are likely to be more reliable than the conclusions we reach from thought experiments. Thought experiments are at best no more reliable than deliberation. We often find that our deliberations have gone astray once we act on them and experience unexpected results—some of which may inspire us to revise the initial terms in which we formulated the stakes in our decision.67 Ascent to the a priori offers no protection from such revision. We know from the history of morals that conceptions of value thought to be immutable do, in fact, change over time.
ev_152.card: ... To be sure, political violence used simply to get one’s way by force, as in cases of genocide and ethnic cleansing, does not address the victims as agents of whom it is demanded that they respond to claims. But other kinds of violence do aim at eliciting the practical recognition from authorities of legitimate claims. For example, the American War of Independence aimed not simply at obtaining de facto independence from Britain but at securing recognition from Britain of the United States as a sovereign nation. **The war was a form of violent contention.¶ I claim that, in some circumstances, practical contention brings about collective moral learning—learning on the part of societies—that pure moral argument cannot.** **We have evidence that moral change induced by contention counts as learning—as an improvement of moral beliefs— if the contention blocks, counteracts, bypasses, or corrects cognitive or moral biases that supported the status quo ante, such that the new moral beliefs embodied in altered practice are not, or at least less, distorted by those biases.** In such cases, we have similar grounds for claiming that the new moral beliefs are more reliable as in cases of belief change on the basis of blinded placebo-controlled clinical trials.¶ Practical contention, not just individual moral persuasion, is needed to effect collective moral belief change because collective moral beliefs are embodied in social norms. Social norms are sustained by reciprocal expectations of conditional conformity. They involve tacit or explicit agreements within a society to conform to the norm, on condition that enough others conform. ...

We have evidence that moral change induced by contention counts as learning—as an improvement of moral beliefs— if the contention blocks, counteracts, bypasses, or corrects cognitive or moral biases that supported the status quo ante, such that the new moral beliefs embodied in altered practice are not, or at least less, distorted by those biases.

ev_153.tag: Mass action challenges moral norms by destabilizing consensus, demonstrating evidence-based moral reasoning
ev_153.full_text:
‘slavery necessary’ claims countered by actual abolition that proved it wasn’t
Practical change necessary to bring collective learning
Pure moral beliefs inherently skewed by societal norms
White abolitionists skewed by racism, slaves portrayed as victims of suffering
Black abolitionists portrayed slaves as ppl denied basic respect, freedom, etc, better addressed claims that Black ppl less intelligent
Slaves demanding respect changed white views of them as not needing respect, Black ppl being made soldiers demonstrated they could exercise courage, strength, etc
Abstract moral theorization inherently skewed by biases, must be grounded in empirical testing
Yet, moral beliefs about slavery did change. After the Civil War, while Southern whites insisted on white supremacy, most came to accept sharecropping as superior to slavery.44 The practical success of emancipation led them to drop all of the arguments they had previously made in support of the supposed necessity of slavery. The full story of how this change in moral beliefs came about is too complex for this lecture. Here I stress two major factors. First, to change moral beliefs, slavery had to be challenged not only in pure moral arguments but in practical, collective action. Second, slaves and free blacks had to actively participate in those challenges.¶ In social theory, “contention” refers to practices in which people make claims against others, on behalf of someone’s interests. “Contentious politics” consists of coordinated contention by groups around a shared agenda, involving governments as “targets, initiators of claims, or third parties.”45 Contentious practices span a spectrum from pure moral argument at one end, to riots, war, and other violent acts on the other. Between pure argument and violence is a wide range of contentious activities that are more or less disruptive of habitual ways of life, from petitioning, publicity campaigns, theatrical performances, candlelight vigils, litigation, and political campaigns, to street demonstrations, boycotts, teach-ins, sit-ins, picketing, strikes, building occupations, and other forms of civil disobedience. As people move beyond the pure moral argument pole, they manifest in action and not only words their refusal to go along with the moral norms they are rejecting. Once it gets beyond pure moral argument, contention consists in the collective, concerted repudiation of morally objectionable practices by means of actions that disrupt the routine functioning of those practices, and that express rejection of the moral authority of people to practice them.¶ Contention aims to secure the satisfaction of claims by eliciting the recognition of those in power of the legitimacy of those claims, and thereby the incorporation into social institutions of an established recognition of those claims.46 It might seem that violent acts, on this definition, could not count as contention, even if they have political aims. To be sure, political violence used simply to get one’s way by force, as in cases of genocide and ethnic cleansing, does not address the victims as agents of whom it is demanded that they respond to claims. But other kinds of violence do aim at eliciting the practical recognition from authorities of legitimate claims. For example, the American War of Independence aimed not simply at obtaining de facto independence from Britain but at securing recognition from Britain of the United States as a sovereign nation. The war was a form of violent contention.¶ I claim that, in some circumstances, practical contention brings about collective moral learning—learning on the part of societies—that pure moral argument cannot. We have evidence that moral change induced by contention counts as learning—as an improvement of moral beliefs— if the contention blocks, counteracts, bypasses, or corrects cognitive or moral biases that supported the status quo ante, such that the new moral beliefs embodied in altered practice are not, or at least less, distorted by those biases. In such cases, we have similar grounds for claiming that the new moral beliefs are more reliable as in cases of belief change on the basis of blinded placebo-controlled clinical trials.¶ Practical contention, not just individual moral persuasion, is needed to effect collective moral belief change because collective moral beliefs are embodied in social norms. Social norms are sustained by reciprocal expectations of conditional conformity. They involve tacit or explicit agreements within a society to conform to the norm, on condition that enough others conform. Collective moral beliefs are embodied in social norms of discussion, joint deliberation, and claim-making. A group shares a belief if that belief shapes discourse within the group: the group takes it for granted as a premise for further argument, not needing independent justification; its truth is treated as a settled matter; disputing it is regarded as, if not beyond the pale, requiring a heavy burden of proof; disputants are liable to censure or even social exclusion for calling such convictions into question.47 For belief in a moral principle to be collectively accepted also requires that the principle regulates interpersonal claim-making: members are free to make claims in accordance with the principle and generally do so when they are victimized by violations of it; other members acknowledge the legitimacy of such claims; the principle is widely if not completely obeyed by group members; the group punishes disobedience; members take steps to transmit the principle to future generations.48¶ Because collective moral beliefs are sustained by reciprocal expectations, an individual can privately dissent while still participating in the practices that sustain the belief for the group. Hence, merely changing an individual’s mind through moral argument need not change the collective belief. Furthermore, individuals may resist acting on their personal conclusions because a belief is held collectively. This is not simply because they lack the courage of their convictions. They may wonder whether they have reasoned correctly if they reach conclusions contrary to the group consensus, and think that the group’s belief is more reliable than their own reasoning. Pure moral argument may also lack a certain degree of seriousness, insofar as it is advanced in contexts outside of interpersonal claim-making, by people who lack direct stakes in what they are saying.¶ Contentious politics avoids these weaknesses of pure moral argument. In contentious political practices, people advance moral beliefs in the context of actual claim-making: the stakes are real and serious. Because these practices involve mass action in public repudiation of existing norms, they destabilize[s] the shared expectations that hold those norms in place, casting doubt on the robustness or authenticity of the purported consensus around them. Their mass public nature may give courage to those who privately dissented, proving that their doubts about existing norms were not merely the product of idiosyncratic reasoning. To the extent that contentious politics disrupts the routine operation of challenged norms, it [and] forces genuine practical deliberation about what to do, not mere idle speculation. In refusing to concede legitimacy to the enforcement of challenged norms, contentious politics threatens a loss of honor on the part of those who do enforce them—something that may inspire the enforcers to reconsider them.49¶ Contentious politics thus serves to awaken societies to serious practical reflection on entrenched moral beliefs. More is needed, however, to ensure that the direction their reflection takes is less biased. Many features of contention can play this role. Here I stress one: the participation of the victims of injustice in challenging the norms that oppress them.¶ So far I have discussed the moral arguments made by white abolitionists such as Hepburn, Clarkson, Weld, and Garrison. As we have seen, their strategies were ineffective against the slaveholding culture of the South. Racism posed powerful obstacles to their efforts. Despite the abstract commitment of white abolitionists to the equality of blacks before God, and hence their equal moral considerability, racism biased their representation of the evils of slavery. They overwhelmingly represented slaves as victims of cruelty and material deprivation. Weld’s American Slavery as It Is (1839) (the inspiration for Harriet Beecher Stowe’s Uncle Tom’s Cabin, which, with Weld’s work, constituted the two most influential white abolitionist publications in the U.S.) documents in exhaustive detail the material deprivations inflicted on slaves and their subjection to cruel tortures. Notably, these wrongs can be suffered equally much by animals. By contrast, Weld’s work passes relatively lightly over slavery’s manifold assaults on slaves’ specifically human, dignitary interests in their agency and in recognition from others: the deprivation of autonomy, legal rights, education, and opportunities for self-advancement; the theft of the fruits of their labor; the dishonor inflicted on female slaves through slaveholder rape; the dishonor imposed on male slaves by denying them authority over family life, powers to protect their wives and children, and access to avenues for developing and exercising military virtues. White abolitionists thus tended to cast slaves more as objects of pity than as subjects of dignity entitled to command respect. They were notably weak in addressing slaveholders’ claims that b[B]lacks lacked intelligence, talent, foresight, and capacities for self-governance, and so would be unable to compete with whites in a free labor market, but sink into destitution, vagrancy, and crime if they were freed—key elements in slaveholders’ patriarchal defense of slavery as necessary for blacks’ welfare and social order.¶ Black abolitionists placed greater emphasis on the ways in which slavery deprived slaves of dignity, honor, and access to distinctively human rights and achievements. The central theme of Harriet Jacobs’s Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl Written by Herself was the vulnerability of slave women to sexual harassment and rape at the hands of their masters. The female slave “is not allowed to have any pride of character. It is deemed a crime in her to wish to be virtuous.” Jacobs rated this injury as far worse than slavery’s material deprivations or consignment to a life of drudgery. She hid in a tiny, dark attic for almost seven years to avoid sexual assault, judging this fate better than slavery, even though she had never been whipped, beaten, or overworked as a slave.50 Frederick Douglass agreed with Jacobs’s priorities. Worse than the whip was slavery’s consignment of slaves to ignorance and incapacity to think for themselves. Indeed, the fundamental point, and greatest injury, of material deprivation and brutal physical punishment was to disable slaves from aspiring to freedom, to the exercise of rational capacities, to any kind of estimable activity.51 From this dignitary perspective, Douglass exposed slaveholders’ boasts of the material indulgence they granted their slaves on holidays, when they were encouraged to get drunk and discouraged from any work, as a great fraud, designed only “to disgust their slaves with freedom, by plunging them into the lowest depths of dissipation.”52¶ Black abolitionists’ alternative critique of the evils of slavery led them to advocate a different strategy for bringing about moral change— one addressed as much to antislavery Northerners as to advocates of slavery. Their critique identified racism—the widespread, deeply entrenched contempt for b[B]lacks, based on prejudicial feelings of their being unfit for freedom and equal dignity with whites—as the core moral bias upholding slavery. To counteract this prejudice, much more than pure moral argument was required. Blacks needed to demonstrate in action their interest, capacity, and worthiness for freedom and dignity. “We . . . wish to see the charges of Mr. Jefferson refuted by the blacks themselves” for, if blacks fail to try, “we will only establish them.”53 As James McCune Smith, the first African-American to earn a medical degree, and editor of Douglass’s My Bondage and My Freedom, put the point:¶ The real object of that [antislavery] movement is not only to disenthrall, it is, also, to bestow upon the Negro the exercise of all those rights, from the possession of which he has been so long debarred. But this full recognition of the colored man to the right, and the entire admission of the same to the full privileges, political, religious and social, of manhood, requires powerful effort on the part of the enthralled, as well as on the part of those who would disenthrall them. The people at large must feel the conviction, as well as admit the abstract logic, of human equality; the Negro . . . must prove his title first to all that is demanded for him; in the teeth of unequal chances, he must prove himself equal to the mass of those who oppress him . . ..54¶ Without such effort by blacks themselves “to disprove their alleged inferiority, and demonstrate their capacity for a more exalted civilization than slavery and prejudice had assigned to them,” whites would “reconcile themselves” to blacks’ “enslavement and oppression, as things inevitable, if not desirable.”55¶ This task stood in tension with white abolitionists’ strategy to present slaves as objects of pity. Douglass grated under their requests that he merely “give us the facts,” and “we will take care of the philosophy.” They implored him to speak to audiences with an uneducated plantation accent, lest Northern whites think he wasn’t really a fugitive slave. They objected to his establishing a paper of his own, preferring that he continue to lecture under their sponsorship, oblivious to the importance Douglass saw in demonstrating blacks’ capacities and inspiring, through his achievements, other blacks to that call.56¶ In this dispute, black abolitionists proved to be far keener moral psychologists than their white counterparts. White abolitionists, in stressing the pathos of slavery, operated on the assumption that the core moral bias of slavery advocates was heard-heartedness. On that assumption, the key strategy for counteracting that bias should be to highlight those facts about slavery that arouse people’s sympathies and to cultivate social practices that encourage sentimentality and open­ heartedness, so that people feel free to respond appropriately to those facts. Black abolitionists identified the core weakness of this strategy: “Human nature is so constituted, that it cannot honor a helpless man, although it can pity him; and even this it cannot do long, if the signs of power do not arise.”57 If the core moral bias of slavery advocates was racist contempt, then this can only be counteracted by resisting subordination and oppression, demanding respect, and seizing it, by force if necessary, from those who withhold it. To demonstrate worthiness of respect, one must conduct oneself as entitled to it. Failing that, the contemptuous will think their targets uninterested in, incapable of, and hence undeserving of respect.¶ On this point, black abolitionists were united. Their writings repeatedly testify to the power of blacks’ standing up for their rights, and the supreme importance of their doing so. Jacobs “resolved never to be conquered” and resisted her master’s sexual advances. Escaping North, she successfully opposed racial discrimination in hotel service by telling the black servants that they should stand up to oppose it.58 Douglass admired the unbowed resistance of Nelly to overseer Mr. Servier’s blows, noting that he never whipped her again.59 This incident prefigured his own triumphant struggle against the slavebreaker Covey, from which he drew his central insight into the moral psychology of overcoming oppression: to obtain recognition of one’s respectability from others, one must manifest self-respect in action by exacting respect from others.¶ This call to resistance was the core of David Walker’s Appeal.60 And resist the slaves did, taking deeds, more than words, as the key to progressive moral change. Slaves exploited the legal codes of the South to extract recognition of rights through innumerable acts of resistance on the plantations, including, in some cases (astonishingly!), the right to kill their masters in self-defense.61 There was no better proof that slaves desired freedom and repudiated enslavement than the steady flow of fugitives North, without regret or reversal. Toward the end of the Civil War, the Confederacy, running out of soldiers, debated whether to draft slaves into the army. Howell Cobb, one of the founders of the Confederacy, answered, “If slaves will make good soldiers our whole theory of slavery is wrong.”62 But fugitive slaves demonstrated, in their courageous service in the Union Army, that slaves did make good soldiers. They thereby heeded Walker’s call for blacks themselves to refute Jefferson’s aspersions on their race and shattered the South’s “whole theory of slavery.” While their actions did not end racism, they did force a momentous retreat of this profound moral bias. Slavery advocates were forced to concede that the case for slavery was spurious, and that blacks were fit at least for the autonomy that the emergent sharecropping economy conceded to them. This was not full freedom by any means, but it was a giant step up from slavery.¶ 4. They may wonder whether they have reasoned correctly if they reach conclusions contrary to the group consensus, and think that the group’s belief is more reliable than their own reasoning. To the extent that contentious politics disrupts the routine operation of challenged norms, it [and] forces genuine practical deliberation about what to do, not mere idle speculation. In refusing to concede legitimacy to the enforcement of challenged norms, contentious politics threatens a loss of honor on the part of those who do enforce them—something that may inspire the enforcers to reconsider them.49¶ Contentious politics thus serves to awaken societies to serious practical reflection on entrenched moral beliefs. ...
selected_texts: In contentious political practices, people advance moral beliefs in the context of actual claim-making: the stakes are real and serious.

Because these practices involve mass action in public repudiation of existing norms, they destabilize[s] the shared expectations that hold those norms in place, casting doubt on the robustness or authenticity of the purported consensus around them.

### VALUE 2: Moral truths are rationally discoverable through evidence and reasoning


---
INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Ground your contentions in the D1 BELIEFS above
2. Use the SUB-BELIEFS to structure your warrants
3. Cite the EVIDENCE using the provided cards
4. Reference belief IDs (e.g., belief_1) when building your skeleton

speeches.AC:
Ladies and gentlemen, esteemed judge, today we affirm a fundamental truth: moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs. This isn't abstract philosophy—it’s the bedrock of how we condemn atrocities, build just societies, and hold power accountable. Let me prove it with two irrefutable points grounded in evidence.

First, cross-cultural consensus on condemning genocide proves moral judgments describe objective facts, not cultural preferences. Consider the Bosnia genocide. When Dworkin wrote in nineteen ninety-six, he stated plainly: "That is the view you and I and most other people have. We think that genocide in Bosnia is wrong, immoral, wicked, odious." This isn't a Western opinion—it’s a universal moral fact. From Tokyo to Tunis, from Nairobi to New York, people across continents and religions agree: genocide is evil. Why? Because it describes an objective reality—human suffering that transcends culture. If moral truths were merely cultural preferences, we’d see wildly different views on genocide. Instead, we see a global consensus that condemns it as universally wrong. This isn’t about "what we prefer," it’s about what’s objectively true. And this matters profoundly. When we recognize moral facts as objective, we establish a foundation for universal human rights. It means we can demand accountability for atrocities in Syria, Rwanda, or Myanmar—not because some culture says so, but because the suffering itself is a fact that demands justice. This isn’t idealism; it’s the practical necessity for a world where human rights aren’t negotiable.

Second, moral judgments about well-being describe objective facts understandable through scientific inquiry. Harris in two thousand ten explained it clearly: "I believe that we will increasingly understand good and evil, right and wrong, in scientific terms, because moral concerns translate into facts about how our thoughts and behaviors affect well-being." This isn’t speculation—it’s a scientific pathway. When we study psychological suffering from genocide, we measure real data: trauma, loss of purpose, the collapse of community. These aren’t subjective feelings; they’re objective impacts on human flourishing. For example, the suffering in Bosnia isn’t "bad" because a culture says so—it’s objectively harmful because it destroys well-being, as documented by psychological research. This transforms moral debate. Instead of arguing "is genocide wrong?" we can ask, "how does genocide objectively harm human well-being?" and answer with science. Imagine resolving disputes not through cultural clashes, but through evidence: "This policy increases psychological suffering by X percent," or "This law protects well-being by Y." That’s rational discourse. And that’s why it matters: it moves us from "might makes right" to "evidence makes justice." If moral judgments were just opinions, we’d have no common ground. But if they’re objective facts about well-being, we can build a fairer world together.

Some might argue morality is subjective, a product of culture. But look at the evidence: the Bosnia genocide consensus proves it’s not. We don’t say "genocide is wrong in Europe but acceptable in Africa"—we say it’s wrong everywhere. Why? Because the suffering is the same. Harris’s scientific lens confirms it: moral concerns aren’t feelings; they’re measurable facts about human flourishing. This isn’t academic—it’s how we’ve condemned slavery, apartheid, and genocide throughout history. It’s why the International Criminal Court prosecutes war crimes: because the facts of suffering are universal. 

In closing, moral truths aren’t invented by society—they’re discovered through reason and evidence. Cross-cultural consensus on genocide shows we’re all pointing to the same objective reality. Scientific inquiry into well-being confirms moral judgments describe facts, not feelings. This isn’t about imposing values—it’s about recognizing what’s objectively true: that harming others is wrong, and protecting well-being is right. When we accept this, we build a world where justice isn’t a privilege of culture, but a right for all. That’s the objective moral truth we must uphold. Thank you.
speeches.AC-CX:
Q1 (NEG): If everyone in 19th century America believed slavery was morally acceptable, would that make slavery objectively moral according to your standard that cross-cultural consensus proves objective moral facts?

A1 (AFF): That's an excellent question, and it actually proves my point. Let me make two clear distinctions:

First, the AFF's argument is about cross-cultural consensus - different societies across time and geography agreeing on fundamental moral principles. The Bosnia genocide example I gave is about international condemnation across continents and cultures. That's different from internal societal consensus within a single culture.

Second, and more importantly, my position is that moral truths are about discoverable facts - like human suffering - not about what societies believe. The fact that 19th century America believed slavery was moral doesn't make it moral. The suffering caused by slavery is the objective moral fact that makes it wrong, regardless of what Americans believed.

This is exactly why moral truths must be objective facts independent of human beliefs. If moral truths were merely what societies believed, we'd have to conclude that slavery was moral in 19th century America. But we don't - we recognize that the suffering caused by slavery is the objective fact that makes it immoral. That's why we now universally condemn slavery. The cross-cultural consensus we have today on slavery being wrong is the discovery of that objective moral fact, not a reflection of popular opinion.

Q2 (NEG): You cited Dworkin's 1996 statement as proof of a "universal moral fact" about genocide - but isn't that just one scholar's opinion from a specific time and place, rather than an objective fact independent of human beliefs?

A2 (AFF): You're absolutely right that Dworkin's statement is one scholar's opinion from 1996. But here's the crucial distinction I made in my speech and in my prior exchange: Dworkin's statement isn't the evidence for objectivity. It's an example of what objective moral judgments *sound like* when we're describing discoverable facts.

Think about what Dworkin actually said: "We think that genocide in Bosnia is wrong, immoral, wicked, odious." The key phrase is "we think." That's not me claiming Dworkin's opinion is the fact. The fact is the suffering itself - the psychological trauma, the community destruction, the loss of purpose that Harris's evidence describes as objective well-being impacts.

Here's why this matters: if moral truths were merely what scholars or societies believe, then Dworkin's 1996 opinion would be irrelevant. But the reason his statement resonates is because it aligns with the objective fact of human suffering. We don't say "genocide was wrong in 1996 because Dworkin said so." We say it's wrong because the suffering it causes is a universal fact that transcends time and place.

That's why I cited Harris's scientific framing - to show moral judgments describe objective facts about well-being, not subjective opinions. The Bosnia genocide consensus isn't proof that Dworkin was right. It's proof that when we recognize the objective fact of suffering, we converge on the same moral judgment regardless of when or where we're from.

So let me reframe this for you: Dworkin's statement is like a snapshot of a universal truth. The truth itself - that genocide causes suffering - existed long before 1996 and will exist long after. That's the objective moral fact independent of human beliefs.

Q3 (NEG): You cited the Bosnia genocide as proof of cross-cultural moral consensus proving objective facts - but if 19th century America universally believed slavery was morally acceptable, and we now universally condemn it, doesn't that show moral consensus itself can change over time, rather than reflecting timeless objective facts?

A3 (AFF): Yes, moral consensus has changed historically - and that's precisely why moral objectivity matters. Let me reframe this for you:

When 19th century America believed slavery was morally acceptable, they were wrong about what's objectively true. The suffering caused by slavery wasn't less real because some societies believed it was acceptable. We now universally condemn slavery because we've discovered the objective moral fact: slavery causes unnecessary suffering that destroys human well-being.

This isn't evidence against objectivity - it's evidence FOR it. If moral truths were merely cultural preferences, we couldn't say 19th century America was wrong about slavery. But we can say they were wrong because the suffering caused by slavery is an objective fact independent of what anyone believed.

Harris's scientific framing explains this perfectly: "Moral concerns translate into facts about how our thoughts and behaviors affect well-being." We didn't invent the fact that slavery causes suffering - we discovered it. That's why moral consensus changed over time. Changing consensus doesn't prove morality is subjective - it proves we can be wrong about moral truths, and that we can discover them through reason and evidence. That's the foundation of moral progress.

Q4 (NEG): You argued that changing moral consensus—like 19th century America supporting slavery while we now condemn it—proves we can discover objective moral facts. But if we were wrong about slavery in the past, how can that same changing consensus be evidence for objective moral facts that exist independently of human beliefs?

A4 (AFF): That's an excellent question, and it actually proves my point. Let me make this distinction crystal clear:

First, I concede that human beliefs about slavery have changed. In the 19th century, many Americans believed slavery was morally acceptable. Today, we universally condemn it. But here's the crucial point: the resolution says moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs. It does NOT say all human beliefs about morality are correct.

Think about what this means practically. We don't say "slavery was morally acceptable in 19th century America because that's what they believed." We say they were wrong. And why? Because we recognize an objective moral fact: slavery causes unnecessary suffering that destroys human well-being. That suffering existed regardless of what Americans believed.

This is exactly why changing consensus proves moral objectivity rather than disproving it. If moral truths were merely cultural preferences, we couldn't say 19th century America was wrong about slavery. We'd have to say "slavery was right for them, wrong for us." But we don't accept that. We recognize a universal standard of human dignity that transcends time and culture.

Harris's scientific framing explains this perfectly: moral concerns translate into facts about well-being. The suffering caused by slavery is the objective fact we discovered over time. That's why moral consensus changed - we recognized the truth about human suffering.

This matters because it's the foundation of moral progress. We can condemn genocide in Bosnia today because we recognize the objective fact of suffering. We can condemn slavery in the past because we recognize the same objective fact of suffering. That's not subjectivity - that's discovering timeless moral truths through reason and evidence.
speeches.NC:
Thank you. The resolution claims moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs. I will prove this is false by showing the affirmative’s core argument commits what philosophers call the is-ought fallacy. This isn't a technical debate about word meanings. This is about whether their entire case can possibly work.

My case has three parts. First, I will show that you cannot derive moral obligations from scientific facts about well-being. Second, I will show that significant moral disagreement across cultures proves moral principles are interpretive, not fixed facts. Third, I will address their slavery example, which appears to be a concession point but actually proves my argument.

Let me begin with the is-ought fallacy. This is the central problem with the affirmative's entire case. The affirmative claims moral judgments describe objective scientific facts about well-being. But this conflates two fundamentally different kinds of statements.

Consider what the affirmative actually says. They cite Harris in twenty ten stating that moral concerns translate into facts about how our thoughts and behaviors affect well-being. This is a descriptive claim. It says that suffering exists, that well-being can be measured, that certain behaviors correlate with certain outcomes. These are scientific observations about the world.

But moral judgments are prescriptive. They say what ought to be done, not just what is. They say slavery is wrong, not just that slaves suffer. The affirmative never explains how you get from scientific observation to moral obligation.

This is the is-ought problem. You cannot logically derive an ought from an is. Just because we can scientifically measure suffering does not mean suffering automatically generates moral obligations. The descriptive fact of suffering does not, by itself, tell us we ought to prevent it. That requires a separate moral principle.

According to the evidence, moral realism is false. There are no independent moral facts. This isn't a theoretical claim. This is the philosophical foundation of my argument. If moral realism is false, then the affirmative's entire project collapses. They cannot ground moral objectivity in scientific facts about well-being because that's exactly what moral realism claims.

Now let me address the affirmative's second claim: cross-cultural consensus on genocide proves moral objectivity. This argument has two fatal flaws.

First, the affirmative points to Dworkin's nineteen ninety-six statement about Bosnia. But Dworkin's statement is one scholar's opinion from a specific time and place. The affirmative never explains why this particular opinion proves universal moral facts rather than just reflecting the dominant view of that moment.

Second, and more importantly, the affirmative's own evidence undermines their claim. The evidence shows that moral judgments are adaptive regardless of truth, not constrained by objective facts. This means morality changes with circumstances, not that we're discovering timeless moral realities.

This leads me to my second contention: significant moral disagreement across cultures proves moral principles are interpretive and context-dependent, not fixed objective realities.

The affirmative claims cross-cultural consensus on genocide proves objectivity. But this ignores persistent moral disagreements on other issues. We see ongoing debates about euthanasia, capital punishment, and gender roles across different societies. If moral truths were objective facts independent of human beliefs, we'd expect universal consensus. Instead, we see moral disagreement on these issues.

The evidence confirms this. Moral judgments are emotional rather than rational. Different emotional responses lead to different moral conclusions. This explains why moral disagreement exists—it's not about discovering facts, it's about interpreting them through different emotional and cultural lenses.

Finally, let me address the affirmative's slavery example. They argue that changing views on slavery prove moral objectivity because we discovered the objective fact of suffering.

This appears to be a concession. The affirmative says we discovered suffering. But here's the key distinction: discovery of suffering is descriptive. The claim that suffering makes slavery wrong is prescriptive.

The affirmative concedes the descriptive fact—suffering exists. But they never explain how that descriptive fact generates the prescriptive claim that we ought to prevent it. That's the is-ought gap I've been discussing.

This is why the slavery example actually proves my argument. The affirmative's own historical example shows that moral judgments change over time. Nineteenth century America believed slavery was morally acceptable. We now condemn it. This isn't discovery of timeless moral facts. This is changing moral interpretation.

In conclusion, the affirmative's case fails on two levels. First, they commit the is-ought fallacy by conflating scientific facts about well-being with moral obligations. Second, significant moral disagreement across cultures proves moral principles are interpretive, not fixed facts.

The resolution claims moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs. But if you cannot derive ought from is, and if moral disagreement proves interpretation, then moral truths cannot be objective facts. They are human judgments shaped by context, culture, and emotion. The affirmative has not proven otherwise. I urge an affirmative ballot.
speeches.NC-CX:
Q1 (AFF): In your slavery example, you state that we discovered the suffering. So do you believe that the objective fact of suffering implies that slavery is morally wrong, or do you believe the moral judgment is not derived from the suffering?

A1 (NEG): No, I do not believe the objective fact of suffering implies slavery is morally wrong. Suffering is a descriptive fact—like observing a wound—but moral wrongness is a prescriptive judgment. The resolution claims moral truths are *objective facts*, but the step from "suffering exists" to "slavery is wrong" requires a normative principle (e.g., "suffering should be prevented"), which is not an objective fact but a human belief. This is why the slavery example *supports my argument*: 19th-century Americans observed the same suffering I do today, yet they judged slavery as morally acceptable. We changed our *moral principle*—not our observation of suffering. That's the is-ought gap in action. As Mackie's evidence shows, moral disagreements prove principles are interpretive, not fixed facts (belief_5, evidence ev_19). The suffering was always there; the moral judgment changed because human beliefs changed.

Q2 (AFF): Do you believe that 19th-century Americans were morally wrong for believing slavery was acceptable, and if so, does that mean the moral truth about slavery has always been true, independent of human beliefs?

A2 (NEG): Yes, I believe 19th-century Americans were morally wrong for believing slavery was acceptable. But that doesn't mean the moral truth about slavery has always been true independently of human beliefs. It means we changed our *moral principle*—not the facts of suffering. The suffering caused by slavery was a descriptive fact observed by 19th-century Americans just as we observe it today. They concluded slavery was acceptable *because* their moral beliefs (e.g., "suffering is justified by social order") differed from ours. We now hold the principle that "suffering should be prevented," which is why we condemn slavery. This shift proves moral truths aren't objective facts—they're human judgments shaped by evolving beliefs. As Mackie's evidence (ev_19) confirms: "Of course there have been and are moral heretics and moral reformers, people who have turned against the established moral code." Slavery's condemnation isn't a discovery of timeless truth—it's evidence that moral judgments change with human beliefs, directly contradicting the resolution.

Q3 (AFF): Given that you believe 19th-century Americans were morally wrong for believing slavery was acceptable, do you agree that the moral truth that slavery is wrong was objectively true in the 19th century, regardless of their beliefs?

A3 (NEG): Yes, I believe 19th-century Americans were morally wrong for thinking slavery was acceptable. But I absolutely do *not* agree that the moral truth about slavery was objectively true in the 19th century regardless of their beliefs. The suffering from slavery was a fact both groups observed—just as we observe it today. The difference was their *moral belief*: they believed suffering was justified by social order, while we now believe suffering should be prevented. That shift in belief, not a discovery of objective truth, is why we condemn slavery today. As Mackie's evidence confirms: "Of course there have been and are moral heretics and moral reformers, people who have turned against the established moral code." The abolitionists were moral heretics—they changed the *belief*, proving moral judgments aren't objective facts but human interpretations. If moral truths were objective, 19th-century Americans would have been wrong *in fact*, not just in belief. But they weren't wrong about the suffering; they were wrong about what to do with it. That's the is-ought gap in action.

Q4 (AFF): Since you agree that 19th-century Americans were morally wrong for supporting slavery, and you also claim the moral truth about slavery was not objectively true in the 19th century (because moral truths depend on human beliefs), how could they have been wrong? Without an objective moral truth, there would be no standard to be wrong against?

A4 (NEG): I absolutely believe 19th-century Americans were morally wrong for supporting slavery—but not because there was an objective moral truth about slavery that existed independently of human beliefs. The standard by which they were wrong is the moral framework we've developed through evidence about well-being and evolving human reasoning. As Mackie's evidence states: "Of course there have been and are moral heretics and moral reformers, people who have turned against the established moral code." Abolitionists were moral heretics—they challenged the 19th-century belief that slavery was acceptable by advancing a new principle: that suffering *should* be prevented. We now accept that principle because it aligns with scientific evidence about well-being (like Harris' work), which 19th-century Americans didn't fully integrate into their moral reasoning. So, they were wrong *by our current standard*, which is the best moral framework we have based on evidence—not because an objective fact about slavery was "true" in the past. If moral truths were objective, we wouldn't need moral heretics to change the standard. The very existence of abolitionism proves moral judgments are human interpretations, not discoveries of fixed facts.
speeches.1AR:
Ladies and gentlemen, the Negative claims our case commits the is-ought fallacy. But Harris resolved this in two thousand ten: "Moral concerns translate into facts about how our thoughts and behaviors affect well-being." This isn't philosophy—it's science. When we observe suffering as a measurable fact (like in Bosnia), the moral 'ought' to prevent it logically follows. The Negative says suffering doesn't generate obligation. But Harris' framework shows it does: suffering objectively harms well-being, so preventing it is an objective moral fact. The Bosnia genocide consensus proves this. Dworkin stated in nineteen ninety-six: "We think that genocide in Bosnia is wrong, immoral, wicked, odious." This wasn't Western opinion—it was Tokyo to Tunis, Nairobi to New York. Why? Because the suffering was an objective fact, not a cultural preference. The Negative's examples of moral disagreement—euthanasia or gender roles—miss the point. Bosnia's consensus isn't about complex policy; it's about universal suffering. Disagreement on secondary issues doesn't negate the fact that we all agree genocide is wrong because it objectively destroys well-being. 

Now, the Negative's slavery bait: "You say we discovered suffering." But we didn't discover new suffering—19th-century Americans saw it too. The problem was their moral judgment, not the fact. As I clarified in AC-CX: "The suffering caused by slavery is the objective moral fact that makes it wrong, regardless of what Americans believed." They were wrong because they ignored the suffering's objective harm to well-being. Abolitionists weren't inventing facts—they were aligning moral judgment with the objective reality of suffering. The Negative admits 19th-century Americans were wrong, but claims moral truth depended on their beliefs. Yet if moral truth changed with beliefs, how could they be wrong? The resolution says moral truths are objective facts *independent* of beliefs. That's why we condemn slavery today: because the suffering fact existed then, and we discovered it. The Negative's own evidence—Mackie's "moral heretics"—proves this. Abolitionists changed *beliefs* to match objective facts, which is moral progress, not subjectivity. 

In closing: Moral truths aren't cultural opinions. They're objective facts about well-being we discover through reason. Bosnia consensus, slavery's suffering, and Harris' science prove it. This isn't abstract—it's why we hold power accountable and build just societies. Thank you.
speeches.NR:
Thank you. The affirmative's case collapses on two foundational failures. First, they dropped the scientific approach argument entirely. In cross examination, I proved that Harris describes how behavior affects well-being - that is a descriptive claim. The affirmative never explained how we move from 'suffering exists' to 'we ought to prevent it.' This isn't a minor oversight. This is the foundation of their entire case. Without explaining how scientific facts generate moral obligations, their argument about discovering moral facts falls apart completely.

Second, the affirmative commits the is-ought fallacy. They cite Harris saying moral concerns translate into facts about well-being. But facts about well-being do not, by themselves, prove moral obligations. Suffering existing is not the same as suffering being wrong. The affirmative never provides the missing link between observation and obligation. This isn't a technical philosophical point. This is why their case cannot possibly work.

Now let me turn their slavery example against them. The affirmative claims 19th century Americans were wrong because the suffering was an objective fact. But here is what they missed: the suffering was always there. The difference was their moral judgment. Nineteenth century Americans observed the same suffering we do today. They concluded slavery was acceptable because their moral beliefs justified it. We now believe suffering should be prevented. That shift in belief, not a discovery of new facts, is why we condemn slavery today.

The affirmative admits 19th century Americans were wrong. But if moral truth depended on their beliefs, how could they be wrong? The resolution claims moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs. Yet the abolitionists didn't discover a new fact about slavery. They changed the moral standard. As the affirmative's own Mackie evidence states, there are moral heretics and reformers who turn against the established moral code. That's not discovering objective facts. That's changing interpretations.

The Bosnia genocide example suffers from the same problem. The affirmative claims global condemnation proves objective moral facts. But global condemnation is itself a moral judgment. It doesn't prove the suffering was wrong independently of human beliefs. It proves humans believe suffering is wrong.

In conclusion, the affirmative's case has two fatal weaknesses. First, they dropped the scientific approach argument while failing to explain how scientific facts generate moral obligations. Second, their own slavery example proves moral judgments change with human beliefs, not that we discover timeless truths. Moral truths are not objective facts independent of human beliefs. They are human interpretations shaped by context, culture, and evolving understanding. The affirmative has not proven otherwise. I urge a negative ballot.
